title: Magnetic Systems and Devices

equations.name: Magnetic flux
equations.notes: cos treated as 1 complex EML node

equations.name: Solenoid field
equations.notes: If mu0*n treated as a single compound constant, cost reduces to 2n

equations.name: Hall voltage
equations.notes: With nqd as compound constant, cost reduces to 3n

equations.name: Force between parallel wires per unit length
equations.formula: F_per_L = mu0 * I1 * I2 / (2 * pi * d)
equations.notes: With mu0/(2*pi) as constant, cost reduces to 5n

equations.name: Magnetic susceptibility (Curie law)
equations.notes: Minimal cost; among the cheapest in all of electromagnetism alongside transformer ratio

equations.name: EMF by Faraday's law (sinusoidal flux)
equations.formula: epsilon = -N * d(B*A*cos(theta(t)))/dt = N * B * A * sin(theta) * (d_theta/dt)
equations.notes: sin counts as 1 complex EML node analogous to cos; total for full sinusoidal flux EMF; dtheta/dt is a terminal input

equations.name: Reluctance and flux in magnetic circuit
equations.formula: R = L / (mu * A); Phi = N * I / R
equations.notes: Reluctance sub-expression: 3n; flux from MMF/reluctance: 3n; combined: 6n

equations.name: Magnetic energy density
equations.notes: With 2*mu0 as compound constant, cost reduces to 4n

equations.name: Lenz's law (induced EMF for solenoid)
equations.notes: dI/dt is a terminal input; very compact

summary_table.name: Force between parallel wires per unit L

key_findings: Curie law (chi = C/T) costs only 1n — the minimum possible non-trivial expression.
Transformer turns ratio costs 3n, making it among the cheapest expressions in electromagnetism.
Solenoid field drops to 2n when mu0*n is a compound constant.
Hall voltage drops from 7n to 3n with a compound nqd constant.
Faraday EMF for sinusoidal flux is the most expensive at 11n, driven by the sin node and the mul chain.
Lenz's law (epsilon = -L * dI/dt) is cheap at 4n because dI/dt is a terminal.
Magnetic energy density benefits from folding 2*mu0 into a constant, saving 2n.
Force between parallel wires is 7n full / 5n with mu0/(2pi) as constant.
